content:
1. Introduction
Bio-oils are an emerging chemical and fuels feedstock derived from a variety of techniques such as the fast pyrolysis of biomass. Phenolic compounds in bio-oils have garnered significant interest for their potential as chemical feedstocks [1–3]. Separating phenolic compounds from bio-oils by typical thermal techniques such as atmospheric distillation is not viable, as the bio-oil compounds polymerize or oxidize at the high temperatures required [4–8]. Instead, extensive solvent extractions are often utilized, which require substantial chemical inventory and pose challenges with solvent recovery and purification [9]. Membrane-based separations such as organic solvent reverse osmosis (OSRO) have the potential to address these difficult separation challenges. These methods use both the differences in size and chemical affinity between the permeating molecules and the membrane, avoiding high temperature operations and the use of hazardous extractants [10,11]. However, while nanofiltration of large molecules from bio-oils has been achieved, translating these approaches to small-molecule bio-oil separations remains an outstanding challenging [12–14]. Bio-oils consist primarily of water together with low concentrations of alcohols, acids, aldehydes, ketones, phenolics, furans, and sugars [15,16]. Strong intermolecular interactions among these species lead to highly nonideal sorption and multicomponent coupling effects, which can diminish size- or affinity-based selectivity in OSRO [17–21]. The presence of many solutes also contributes to high osmotic pressure, which necessitates higher operating pressures and makes the separation more energy intensive. Osmotically assisted reverse osmosis (OARO) has drawn interest in this regard because it can partially alleviate osmotic pressure limitation by using a draw solution to reduce the required hydraulic pressure [22–28].

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content: A separate challenge arises from the dilute nature of many high-value bio-oil solutes. At low concentrations, the driving force becomes weak, thus limiting permeation fluxes and separation performance. When solutes are present at low concentrations, the concentration gradient across the membrane becomes small, limiting the driving force for permeation [18–21]. In membrane process design, the usual response is to selectively reject the dilute solute so that it accumulates in the retentate, as in OARO. However, achieving meaningful concentration in this way requires a large volume of solvent to permeate the membrane, making the process area-intensive. If instead a membrane preferentially permeates a dilute solute relative to the bulk solvent, the solute can accumulate in the permeate even from an initially lower concentration, an effect commonly described as "uphill" permeation [29]. Because enrichment occurs in the permeate stream, this mode can reduce the volumetric solvent load that must pass through the membrane to achieve a comparable degree of solute concentration. Uphill transport phenomena have been documented in several membrane and biological systems. In charged nanofiltration membranes, for example, permeation of dilute species can occur when electroneutrality or Donnan potentials drive certain ions toward the permeate more strongly than water [30]. Also, biological channels similarly move molecules against concentration gradients by coupling transport to external chemical or electrochemical energy sources [31].

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content: In polymer membranes operating under fully dissipative, neutral conditions, however, uphill permeation is much less common [32–34]. In organic solvent nanofiltration (OSN), Postel and co‑workers reported negative retention of neutral solutes such as n‑alkanes and linear carboxylic acids when alcohols were used as solvents [35]. They attributed this behavior to solute‑membrane affinity and competitive sorption effects, demonstrating that non‑ionic "uphill" transport can emerge from sorption thermodynamics alone, without electrostatic forces, co‑reactants, or external energy coupling. This sorption‑driven uphill permeation can be described by the "sorpvection" framework introduced by Damle and Koros, later examined experimentally by White et al. in water and dimethylformamide binary system and further developed recently by Kim et al. [36–38]. In this mechanism, a strongly sorbing dilute solute is carried through the membrane by the "convective" flux of another permeant, allowing it to enrich on the permeate side despite an opposing concentration or fugacity gradient. These studies firmly establish that neutral solutes can permeate uphill in polymer membranes through coupled sorption and convective transport.

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content: What remains largely unexplored is whether sorpvection exists in complex, multicomponent liquids, where competitive sorption, strong hydrogen bonding, and co‑transport effects become dominant. Most prior observations of sorpvection involve binary or otherwise idealized systems: whether this process occurs in complex, non‑binary liquids remains untested, as does its practical relevance for real‑world application. In practice, bio‑oils are multicomponent, aqueous‑organic mixtures in which competitive sorption and co‑transport phenomena within the membrane are expected to dominate. If the mechanism applies, some molecules would pass through the membrane by sorbing into it and being swept along by more permeable species. Such behavior could, in principle, concentrate dilute phenolics from bio‑oil without requiring large solvent throughput. Here, we examine sorpvection in model and synthetic bio‑oil mixtures using a spirocyclic aromatic‑rich polytriazole (DUCKY‑9) thin‑film composite membrane. A five‑component mixture (water/methanol/acetol/guaiacol/glucose) was selected because it captures the major functional groups found in pyrolysis oils while allowing controlled evaluation of competitive sorption and transport [39,40]. We first examine phenolic transport under steady‑state permeation with this five‑component feed, then vary ternary (water/methanol/guaiacol) feeds to explore coupling effects. Flux‑flux analysis is paired with sorption‑isotherm measurements on DUCKY‑9 to connect membrane‑phase composition with observed coupling phenomena. We then consider the process implications through a simple OARO cascade analysis, showing how the sorpvection concept could translate into reduced membrane area.

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
2. Materials and methods
2.1. Materials
DUCKY‑9 polymer was synthesized following previously reported procedures on a 4–8 g scale (5–10 mmol in monomer) [41]. Matrimid® 5218 was obtained from Huntsman Corporation. Commercial membranes used as controls (Puramem Performance and oNF2) were purchased from Sterlitech Corporation. Chemicals and solvents used in this work (including lithium nitrate, p‑xylylenediamine, hexane, N‑methyl‑2‑pyrrolidone, tetrahydrofuran, methanol, ethanol, chloroform, acetol, guaiacol, glucose and polyethylene glycol 400) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich or VWR and used as received unless otherwise noted.

2.2. DUCKY‑9 thin‑film composite (TFC) fabrication
The crosslinked Matrimid support was prepared by mixing a dope solution composed of Matrimid®, N‑methyl‑2‑pyrrolidone, tetrahydrofuran, ethanol, de‑ionized water, and lithium nitrate (weight ratio = 16:69:10:3:1:1) on a rotating roller. The dope was then cast onto a glass plate using a 10 MIL casting blade and transferred to a deionized water bath after 9 s. The resulting film was sequentially solvent‑exchanged in water (1 day), methanol (1 h), and hexane (1 h), with each step repeated three times, and subsequently punched into 3.8 cm‑diameter circular coupons. For crosslinking, 50 support coupons were immersed in a solution of 5 g p‑xylylenediamine in 100 mL methanol for 24 h. Following crosslinking, the support underwent solvent exchange with methanol and hexane three times each to remove any residual solvents and diamine. The dope for the DUCKY‑9 TFC was prepared by dissolving 1 wt% DUCKY‑9 polymer in chloroform, filtering the solution through 0.22 μm PTFE syringe filters, and cooling to 277 K. DUCKY‑9 TFCs of approximately 500 nm were prepared by spin‑coating 0.7 mL of dope onto a crosslinked Matrimid support at 1000 rpm for 2 min. The structural and performance characterizations of DUCKY‑9 TFC membranes have been reported in detail elsewhere [41–43]; here, we present only the membrane performance and sorption data relevant to this work.

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
2.3. Liquid permeation test
A custom‑built crossflow system capable of maintaining high pressure flow by an HPLC pump (Azura P 4.1S, Knauer) was used [44]. Before testing, the membranes were pre‑soaked in the feed mixture overnight to ensure consistent swelling during the measurement. Each membrane was mounted in the crossflow permeation cells, and then the system was circulated with the feed solution at 40 bar, and it remained under these operating conditions for one week prior to measurement. The permeate mass was then recorded over time to determine the hydraulic permeance (Pi/l ), calculated as Pi/l = mi/(ρi·A·t·Δp), where mi and ρi are the mass and density of component i in the permeate, respectively, A is the membrane area, t is the permeate collection time, and Δp is the transmembrane hydraulic pressure. All measurements were performed at room temperature (295 K) and 40 bar transmembrane pressure, with a feed flow rate of 15 mL/min for mixtures without glucose and 5 mL/min or 20 mL/min for mixtures containing glucose (the latter used to assess concentration polarization). The stage cut (permeation rate over feed flow rate) was kept below 1 %. With the exception of the first experiment (81:19 mol% methanol:guaiacol, N=1) in the ternary separation experiments, every reported value represents the mean of at least two replicate membranes. The collected permeates were analyzed by gas chromatography (Agilent 7890B, equipped with a thermal conductivity detector and 30 m DB‑Wax capillary column) against calibration curves for each analyte. For glucose concentration analysis, the volatile components were removed using a rotary evaporator, leaving the glucose residue for measurement. Glucose concentration was then determined gravimetrically by dividing the mass of the dried residue by the mass of the original permeate sample.

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
Selectivity was expressed either as the concentration ratio between the permeate (Cp) and retentate (Cr), or as the separation factor of component i over component j (SFi/j). Because the stage cut in all permeation experiments was kept below 1 %, the retentate composition (Cr) remained effectively equal to the feed composition under our operating conditions. The separation factor is given by SFi/j = (Cj,r/Ci,r) / (Cj,p/Ci,p), where Ci and Cj represent the concentrations of components i and j in the retentate (r) or the permeate (p). For the N<5 datasets, selectivity values (Cp/Cr ratios or separation factors) were reported as the mean ± standard deviation. For the N=5 dataset, the selectivity data were log‑transformed and tested against the null hypothesis of no selectivity (Cp/Cr=1, SF=1) using one‑sample t‑tests [45]. The data are presented as geometric means and 95 % confidence interval (CI), calculated from log‑transformed values and back‑transformed to the ratio scale. Statistical significance was evaluated at α=0.05.

2.4. Sorption measurement
A manual gravimetric sorption experiment was designed to measure the mass fraction of guaiacol in the membrane as a function of activity. A pan containing polymer powder was suspended on a pre‑weighed vial cap inside a sealed vial positioned above a liquid mixture of guaiacol and polyethylene glycol 400 (PEG 400) at 343 K to ensure guaiacol vapor saturation. The activity of guaiacol was controlled using PEG 400, which, due to its low volatility, is assumed to not meaningfully partition into the vapor phase and sorb into the polymer but remains miscible with guaiacol. After five days of exposure at each activity point, the mass change was determined by immediately transferring the vial cap and pan to an empty vial of known weight and recording the total weight. The vapor sorption isotherms of water and methanol in the DUCKY‑9 polymer were measured using a gravimetric vapor sorption instrument VTI‑SA+ (TA instrument, New Castle, DE) at 298 K. Before the sorption process, the samples were dried at 383 K for 12 h under a nitrogen flow to remove any residual moisture and solvents. Sorption isotherms were obtained by incrementally increasing the vapor activity and allowing the sample mass to equilibrate at each step (mass change rate below 0.01 wt% over 30 min). For water sorption, measurements were conducted at vapor activities of 0.10, 0.20, 0.35, 0.50, 0.60, and 0.75; for methanol sorption, at 0.10, 0.30, 0.50, 0.60, and 0.70. The sorption coefficient Si as a function of activity was then determined from the isotherm data using the local slope: Si = dqi/dai, where qi is the sorption uptake and ai is the activity of component i.

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
2.5. Solubility measurement
To construct a ternary phase diagram for water, methanol, and guaiacol, various mixtures with different solvent compositions were prepared. If a mixture did not exhibit two‑phase separation or cloudiness after shaking, 1 mL of guaiacol was added to the test tube. The mixture was then vortexed for 5 min. This process was repeated until the mixture turned cloudy. All solubility experiments were conducted at room temperature (approximately 295 K).

2.6. Fugacity calculations
The driving force for diffusive flux in OSRO membrane separations is the fluid phase transmembrane fugacity gradient (Δffl). The fugacity of a component in a fluid (feed or permeate) was calculated as ffl = xi·γi·psat_i·exp[Vi·(p - psat_i)/(RT)], where xi is the mole fraction, γi is the activity coefficient, Vi is the partial molar volume (assumed to be the pure component molar volume) of component i, R is the gas constant, T is the system temperature, p is the pressure of liquid phase, and psat_i is the saturated vapor pressure of component i at a given temperature. We used the SR‑POLAR model in ASPEN to calculate the activity coefficients given the high polarity of the bio‑oil feed components.

2.7. Reproducibility
The DUCKY‑9 TFC membranes used to evaluate separation performance for the 5‑component model mixture were fabricated using the procedure described above. Approximately 30 % of these membranes were deemed defective, as indicated by markedly lower glucose rejection and significantly higher permeances (more than 3 standard deviations away from the other membranes). We speculate that guaiacol's high solubility in both DUCKY‑9 and Matrimid polymers likely caused excessive swelling in the selective and support layer, resulting in minor defects and insufficient mechanical strength to obtain reliable data for this population of failed membranes.

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
3. Results and discussion
3.1. Separation performance of DUCKY‑9 membranes in a model bio‑oil mixture
The primary objective of this work is to better understand the mass transport modality by investigating the permeation of phenolics through DUCKY‑9 thin film composite membranes. A simplified bio‑oil model mixture consisting of water, methanol (alcohol), acetol (ketone), guaiacol (phenolic), and glucose (sugar) was prepared in the following molar compositions: 72 % water, 14 % methanol, 6 % acetol, 5 % guaiacol, and 3 % glucose. These compounds were chosen because they represent the dominant chemical families in typical bio‑oils, with guaiacol and glucose specifically included as representative phenolic and sugar species that exhibit strong and contrasting sorption behavior in polymer membranes [39,40]. Fig. 2 shows the separation results of the model bio‑oil mixture using five DUCKY‑9 TFC membranes, with two silicon‑based commercial OSN membranes (Puramem Performance and oNF2) included for comparison. oNF2 exhibited high permeance but essentially no separation capability, with Cp/Cr values close to 1 for all components, consistent with its reported molecular weight cut‑off range (250–450 Da). In contrast, Puramem Performance showed typical size‑based separation: the largest solute, glucose, was rejected (Cp/Cr = 0.31±0.04), while methanol, acetol, and guaiacol permeated in proportion to their molecular size (smaller molecules more enriched in the permeate). However, water was partially rejected, despite its smallest molecular size in terms of molar mass. DUCKY‑9 TFC membranes rejected the largest molecule, glucose, concentrating it on the retentate side (Cp/Cr = 0.25, 95% CI: 0.10–0.69). In contrast, guaiacol, despite its low concentration (5 mol%) in the feed and comprising the second largest molecule in the mixture, was enriched in the membrane permeate (Cp/Cr = 1.50, 95% CI: 1.11–2.04).

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
As most OSRO and reverse osmosis permeation systems exhibit "downhill" permeation (solute permeates from higher to lower concentration), the observation of preferential permeation and enrichment of guaiacol in the membrane permeate side is unusual. An unexpectedly large separation factor of 5.8 (95% CI: 1.7–20) was observed between glucose and guaiacol, which have a molecular weight difference of less than 60 Da. The relatively large variance in the separation factor arises because this parameter becomes increasingly sensitive to small compositional fluctuations as the mole fraction of glucose in the permeate decreases. For comparison, the guaiacol/glucose separation factor for oNF2 was 1.1±0.1 and for Puramem was 3.5±0.8. These results indicate that DUCKY‑9 operates through a separation mechanism distinct from conventional size‑exclusion behavior. The total permeance for this mixture through DUCKY‑9 ((2.0±0.7)×10⁻³ L m⁻² h⁻¹ bar⁻¹) was comparable to that of Puramem Performance but significantly lower than oNF2. Improvement of this parameter can be expected from changes in membrane thickness, pump flow rate, or higher operating temperature, among other engineering approaches.

3.2. Effect of guaiacol concentration in membrane feed and guaiacol sorption
To further investigate this unusual separation phenomenon, we simplified the feed to include only water, methanol, and guaiacol and gradually diluted the guaiacol by addition of water during permeation experiments, allowing the membranes to reach steady state after each addition. Two sets of experiments were conducted: one beginning with a feed containing 81 mol% methanol and 19 mol% guaiacol (4.3:1 mol/mol), and the other starting with 92 mol% methanol and 8 mol% guaiacol (11.5:1 mol/mol). In both cases, the methanol:guaiacol ratio was kept approximately constant while water was gradually introduced. As Fig. 4 illustrates, guaiacol was rejected (Cp/Cr < 1) when its concentration in the feed was high; after the feed was diluted, Cp/Cr flipped above 1 and guaiacol became enriched in the permeate – a trend opposite to the downhill transport normally expected for conventional membrane transport processes [46,47].

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
We surmise that this behavior derives from the favorable sorption behavior of guaiacol in the low activity region (0.02–0.15) relevant to our system compared to that of water. The sorption of guaiacol is much more sensitive to activity than that of water: the derivative‑based sorption coefficient rises sharply for guaiacol as its activity falls, whereas the sorption coefficient of water remains nearly constant in the activity range of interest. Consequently, the ratio of sorption coefficient of guaiacol over water (Sguaiacol/Swater, the guaiacol‑to‑water sorption selectivity) increases continuously with decreasing activity of guaiacol. This growing sorption selectivity enriches guaiacol in the permeate even though its absolute flux decreases with declining feed activity (or concentration). Also, considering that the sorption test for guaiacol was conducted at a markedly higher temperature (343 K) compared to that for water (298 K), and that sorption is inherently an exothermic process, it is reasonable to expect that the actual sorption selectivity between water and guaiacol in the conducted membrane permeation tests would be greater than that observed from the sorption isotherm measurements. This preferential permeation occurs with a basically zero or even negative transmembrane fugacity of guaiacol, indicating that guaiacol permeated through the membrane even in the absence of a chemical potential (or fugacity) driving force.

3.3. Flux coupling effects
In sorpvection transport, the fluxes of the various components are thought to couple together, and this coupling can be represented by summing the diffusive flux of an individual component with a product of averaged mass fraction and total flux of all compounds permeating through the membrane: ni = ji + ωi·ntotal, where ni is the flux of component i across a membrane on a mass basis, ji is the diffusive flux of component i, ωi is the average mass fraction of component i in the membrane, and ntotal is the overall total flux through the membrane [37,48]. The permeation of guaiacol through the membrane despite no transmembrane fugacity gradient definitively implies the absence of purely diffusive flux of guaiacol (i.e., jguaiacol = 0). Removal of the diffusive flux term defines the flux of guaiacol to be equal to the "convective" flux: nguaiacol ≅ ωguaiacol·ntotal.

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content: Our experiments indicate that guaiacol transport is governed by sorpvection modality where bulk flow dominates. Equation nguaiacol ≅ ωguaiacol·ntotal suggests a linear correlation between guaiacol flux and total flux, which is directly testable. Fig. 6A shows such a correlation to a reasonable degree (R² = 0.83). The slope of the flux‑flux plot corresponds to the average mass fraction of guaiacol (ωguaiacol) within the membrane during separation. Our results provide a slope of approximately 0.26±0.05, suggesting that the average mass fraction of guaiacol in the membrane over the tested conditions is 26±5 wt%. The loading of guaiacol in the membrane was measured using gravimetric sorption experiments. During the permeation experiments (Fig. 6A), the activities of guaiacol in the feed mixture ranged from 0.02 to 0.15. Gravimetric sorption experiments show guaiacol mass fraction in DUCKY‑9 of 0.139±0.015 to 0.359±0.050 kg/kg in that activity range. Assuming a linear concentration profile of guaiacol across the membrane with feed and permeate activities of 0.02 and 0.15, we estimate the average loading of guaiacol in the membrane during permeation to be 24.9±3.2 wt%. This compares reasonably well with the value from permeation data (26±5 wt%), providing tentative evidence supporting the sorpvection modality.

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
Equation (6) does not explicitly specify which compounds are coupled to each other. To examine this, the guaiacol flux and fluxes of methanol and water were compared (Fig. 6B and C). The correlation with methanol is moderate rather than perfectly linear (R² = 0.70). This can be expected in a ternary feed where competitive sorption with water introduces variability, and the relatively low permeance of guaiacol increases scatter. Despite these effects, the stronger correlation with methanol (R²=0.70) compared to water (R²=0.37) supports the conclusion that methanol is the dominant sorpvecting agent in the DUCKY‑9 membranes. We surmise that guaiacol molecules are coupled with methanol instead of water due to differences in solubility. In the ternary phase diagram of water, methanol, and guaiacol, as methanol concentration increases more guaiacol can be dissolved, whereas increasing water concentration does not significantly affect guaiacol solubility. In fact, guaiacol/methanol mixtures concentrated in water can phase separate, indicating that guaiacol is highly soluble in methanol but poorly soluble in water. Furthermore, according to the squared solubility difference (Ra) – a measure of chemical affinity between the polymer and solvents – guaiacol in methanol has a value three times lower than in water, indicating guaiacol's strong preference for methanol compared to water. Therefore, we speculate that sorpvection‑type separation occurs as follows: guaiacol first selectively sorbs into the membrane upstream due to its high solubility in the membrane materials and then couples to sorbed methanol, which exhibits a high driving force for permeation. The high diffusivity and driving force for methanol imbue it with relatively high fluxes, which ultimately provides meaningful uphill fluxes for the coupled guaiacol. The net entropy generated during the permeation experiments was consistently positive, demonstrating that the proposed sorpvection modality does not violate the second law.

3.4. Enhancing guaiacol‑glucose selectivity by engineering sorpvection
The ultimate goal of this work is to better understand sorpvection transport so that this modality can be engineered to improve targeted separations. Fig. 2 shows that while guaiacol is primarily concentrated on the permeate side, glucose is highly concentrated on the retentate side [51]. Since methanol does not significantly affect glucose solubility, supplementing the feed mixture with methanol should facilitate guaiacol permeation while ensuring glucose remains rejected, thereby enhancing guaiacol/glucose selectivity. Also, methanol does not present the same thermal‑sensitivity issues as heavier bio‑oil components; because of its low boiling point (64.7 °C) and thermal stability, the small amount added (5 mol%) can be readily removed by low‑temperature flash evaporation [52,53].

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
To evaluate this effect, crossflow permeation experiments were performed using the five‑component mixture (water, methanol, guaiacol, acetol, glucose). After each run, the methanol content in the feed was increased by ~5 mol% (from initial 20 mol% to 29 mol%) with 24 h allowed for the membrane to reach steady state while the ratios of the other components were kept constant. Fig. 7 shows that increasing the methanol content by only 5 mol% markedly enhanced the guaiacol/glucose separation factor by ~2.6 times (from 5.4 to 13.8), accompanied by a slight increase in permeance. Thus, this sorpvection‑based membrane system raised the guaiacol‑to‑glucose ratio by 14‑fold relative to the feed mixture. The variability observed in Fig. 7 mainly arises from membrane swelling and concentration polarization effects. Both DUCKY‑9 and Matrimid swell significantly in guaiacol, resulting in area‑based swelling degrees of 16.4% even in feed mixtures with low guaiacol concentration. Such swelling slightly changes the membrane's apparent thickness and internal structure between runs, leading to variations in selectivity. In addition, concentration polarization can amplify small experimental differences. When solutes like glucose accumulate near the membrane surface, they increase local viscosity and osmotic pressure, reducing the effective driving force. Consequently, small changes in flow rate or viscosity can cause noticeable fluctuations in flux, contributing to the data scatter.

3.5. Impacts of concentration polarization
The main limitation in this study is the low total permeance of ~10⁻³ L m⁻² h⁻¹ bar⁻¹. This was attributed to severe concentration polarization, as the bio‑oil feed contained substantial amounts of guaiacol and glucose (18 and 15 wt%, respectively), which increased density and viscosity (~1.4 cP). The heavy, viscous feed was also difficult for our HPLC pump to circulate, and the maximum stable flow rate was limited to 5 mL/min, conditions that further exacerbated polarization at the membrane surface. When a newer but equivalent pump capable of higher flow rates was used, stable operation at 20 mL/min was achieved, and permeance rose ~6.4‑fold to ~10⁻² L m⁻² h⁻¹ bar⁻¹ (Fig. 8A). This outcome indicates that concentration polarization is in part responsible for the low permeance and that higher feed flow can effectively mitigate this limitation. At higher flow, sorpvection effects were also more pronounced, with guaiacol permeation further enhanced by reduced concentration polarization and greater methanol flux, achieving a higher guaiacol‑glucose selectivity. The guaiacol/glucose separation factor increased ~19% from 5.8 (95% CI: 1.7–20) to 6.9±3.3 with reduced variance, indicating improved consistency in membrane performance (Fig. 8B).

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
3.6. Using sorpvection in membrane cascades
OARO is a promising and energy‑efficient technique to recover diluted compounds from solvent mixtures. The most common example is the recovery of salt from seawater [22,25]. Conceptually, this technique can also be utilized to recover solutes such as guaiacol from dilute mixtures [28]. One challenge with OARO is that significant membrane areas are needed to recover relatively small amounts of the diluted compound, making this technology energy efficient but capital intensive [27]. To compare sorpvection membranes with the conventional reverse osmosis (RO) membrane that is typically installed ahead of an OARO cascade to pre‑concentrate the feed by rejecting the solute, we adopted the operating conditions reported by González et al. for their high‑recovery OARO cascade [27]. In that work, each OARO stage removed 10.3% of its retentate as product and recycled an internal stream equal to 1.04 times that product flow (reflux ratio). All membrane passes were run at a 20% stage cut. The feed rate was fixed at 1000 m³ h⁻¹ containing 15 kg m⁻³ of the target solute (e.g., guaiacol). Under these assumed conditions, the cascade would yield a final guaiacol‑rich product with a flow of 103 m³ h⁻¹ at a concentration of 142.7 kg m⁻³, corresponding to ~98% solute recovery from the feed and an overall enrichment factor of ~9.5 relative to the feed concentration. In the OARO‑RO cascade, the OARO product permeate (diluted in the solute) is sent to an RO pre‑concentration unit. This RO unit is assumed to reject 90% of the solute such that the concentrated RO retentate (3588 m³ h⁻¹) merges with the fresh feed. The resulting combined stream is 4588 m³ h⁻¹, nearly 4.6 times the original flow, and all of it must pass through the OARO cascade, requiring a commensurate increase in membrane area and thus capital costs. We investigated the impact of the pre‑concentration achievable by the sorpvection unit by varying the solute concentration factor (permeate concentration divided by feed concentration) from 1.5 to 5. If concentration factors of five are possible, replacing the RO pre‑concentration unit with a sorpvection membrane operated at a similar stage cut (19.6%) reverses the flow pattern. The sorpvection unit creates a small, highly concentrated permeate that recycles to the feed, while the much larger but dilute retentate is withdrawn. Consequently, only a modest increase over the original 1000 m³ h⁻¹ reaches the OARO train, and the cascade now needs to process just 1218 m³ h⁻¹ of feed to deliver the same product flow at a comparable solute concentration. Because required OARO membrane area scales almost linearly with permeate throughput, coupling sorpvection cuts that area to about 3.8 times lower than the RO‑OARO system. If sorpvection membranes with sufficiently high permeance become available, they could serve as an efficient pretreatment to OARO, significantly decreasing the volumetric flow that downstream OARO stages must handle, and consequently reducing the overall membrane area required for solute recovery.

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes

content:
4. Conclusions
The use of thin‑film composite DUCKY‑9 membranes to separate the components of a model bio‑oil mixture provided enrichment of guaiacol and rejection of glucose in the permeate, a separation behavior unique to this hydrophobic, aromatic‑rich membrane and not observed in the silicon‑based commercial nanofiltration membranes tested. By examining the relationship between fluxes, solubilities, and sorption, we found that guaiacol transport in DUCKY‑9 membrane follows the sorpvection modality, with methanol acting as the sorpvecting agent. This coupling improved the guaiacol/glucose selectivity by 2.6‑fold and increased the guaiacol‑to‑glucose ratio in the permeate by 14‑fold relative to the feed. At faster pump flow, the sorpvection effect became even more pronounced, further increasing the guaiacol/glucose selectivity by 19%.
Translating this finding to practical application will require addressing the low permeance of the DUCKY‑9 sorpvecting membrane (~10⁻³ L m⁻² h⁻¹ bar⁻¹). Methanol serves as an effective sorpvecting agent and increases permeance, and higher crossflow rates further raised permeance to ~10⁻² L m⁻² h⁻¹ bar⁻¹. This indicates that the low permeance observed at lab scale is largely due to concentration polarization and could be alleviated under industrial operating conditions. Beyond concentration polarization issues, the application of the sorpvection mechanism to more complex mixtures may be inhibited by the need to identify couplings between components, and sorpvection may not be advantageous if the coupled flux facilitates the transport of undesired solutes. Nonetheless, our process analysis suggests that if sorpvection membranes with higher permeance can achieve about 5 times greater solute concentration in the permeate, coupling sorpvection as a pretreatment could reduce the downstream OARO membrane area by approximately 3.8 times. In summary, this work demonstrates uphill transport of phenolics in multicomponent mixtures, identifies methanol‑driven sorpvection as the mechanism, and shows how such flux coupling can potentially be leveraged to lower capital requirements in membrane cascades, provided low flux limitations can be addressed.

citation.title: The unusual transport of phenolic compounds through poly(triazole) membranes
